structure.title: Configuration 152
structure.summary: The section titled "Configuration 152" (likely a reference to pages 154-154) discusses two subsections: "Default Values" and "Semantic Constraints." "Default Values" outlines the pre-set parameters for system configurations, while "Semantic Constraints" defines rules governing the interpretation and validity of configuration data. These subsections collectively establish baseline settings and logical conditions to ensure consistent and meaningful configuration management. The text specifies page numbers 154 for both subsections, indicating they are part of a larger documentation set. No additional technical details beyond these subsections are provided.

structure.title: Introduction and functional overview
structure.summary: This specification defines the functionality, API, and configuration for the `Raw Data Stream` functional cluster in the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform, enabling applications to process raw binary data streams over communication channels. It distinguishes between two scenarios: (1) byte streams, where data is untyped and requires no serialization, and (2) datagram streams, where Ethernet frames are treated as atomic units, with header information processed as typed data and payloads as

structure.title: Raw Data Streaming interface is statically defined and dependent on the underlying network protocol. The modeling for the Raw Data Streaming Interface supports the following network protocols:
structure.summary: The Raw Data Streaming interface is statically defined and protocol-dependent, supporting two network protocols: IP-based (network layer) and IEEE1722 (data link layer). For IP-based streams, TCP/IP sockets (transport layer) use TCP (reliable) or

structure.title: Acronyms and Abbreviations
structure.summary: The section provides a glossary of acronyms and abbreviations specific to this specification. It notes that these terms are only relevant within the context of the current document. A general list of acronyms and abbreviations is referenced in [2], indicating additional resources for broader terminology. The glossary does not include terms from external documents or standards. All listed acronyms and abbreviations are tied to the scope of this particular specification.

nodes.title: MAC unicast socket connection
nodes.summary: A MAC unicast socket connection is a communication method using a socket provided by the operating system, bound to the data link layer (ISO OSI layer 2). It employs a media access control (MAC) address as the destination identifier, enabling direct communication with exactly one remote peer (unicast). The MAC address operates at the data link layer, ensuring point-to-point connectivity. This mechanism leverages the OSI model's second layer for addressing and data transmission. The unicast nature guarantees exclusive communication between the socket and a single target

nodes.title: MAC multicast socket connection
nodes.summary: A "MAC multicast socket connection" refers to communication through an OS-provided socket bound to the data link layer (ISO OSI layer 2). It utilizes a media access control (MAC) address to enable multicast communication, allowing data transmission to one or more counter communication partners simultaneously. The socket operates at the data link layer, bypassing higher-layer protocols to directly address multicast MAC addresses. This method leverages Layer 2 network capabilities for efficient, broadcast-like communication within a local network segment. The term emphasizes the use of MAC addresses for

nodes.title: AVTPDU-common-header
nodes.summary: The AVTPDU-common-header, defined by [1, IEEE1722], consists of the first

nodes.title: AVTPDU-common-stream-header
nodes.summary: The "AVTPDU-common-stream-header" is defined by [1, IEEE1722] and extends the AVTPDU-common-header used by a subset of AVTP stream data sub-types. It is specifically employed by sub-types such as 61883_IIDC, AAF, TSCF, and RVF. The header provides additional fields or functionality to support these specific data sub-types within the AVTP protocol framework. The expansion ensures compatibility and structured transmission of stream data across different AVTP applications. The definition explicitly references IEEE1722 as the standard defining this header structure.

nodes.title: AVTPDU-common-control-header
nodes.summary: The AVTPDU-common-stream-header is defined by [1, IEEE1722] and expands the AVTPDU-common-header. It is specifically used by a subset of AVTP stream data subtypes, such as ADP. The header provides additional control information for these subtypes, extending the functionality of the base AVTPDU-common-header. The definition is tied to IEEE1722 standards, ensuring compatibility with AVTP stream protocols. No other subtypes or functionalities are mentioned in the text.

nodes.title: AVTPDU-alternative-header
nodes.summary: The AVTPDU-alternative-header is defined by [1, IEEE1722] and serves AVTP stream data subtypes that lack the commonalities shared by formats using AVTPDU-common-stream-header or AVTPDU-common-control-headers. It is specifically employed for subtypes like CRF and NTSCF, which do not conform to the standard headers used for other AVTP data formats. This header provides an alternative structure to accommodate unique formatting requirements of these subtypes. The distinction highlights the need for specialized headers when common header mechanisms are insufficient. The definition explicitly ties its use to subtypes that diverge from the shared characteristics of other AVTP data formats.

nodes.title: AVTPDU-payload
nodes.summary: The AVTPDU-payload is the second component of an AVTPDU, as defined by [1, IEEE1722], and carries data corresponding to the subtype encoded in the AVTPDU-header. It serves as the payload container for specific data types, such as audio samples in the case of an AAF-payload. The payload's content is determined by the subtype field in the AVTPDU-header, ensuring data is transmitted according to the specified format. Examples include audio data carried by AAF-payloads, illustrating its role in transporting media streams. The structure ensures alignment between header subtypes and payload data types for protocol compliance.

nodes.title: Stream data producer
nodes.summary: A "stream data producer" is an end node in an Ethernet network that continuously generates data transmitted via a stream. This data is received by one or multiple end nodes acting as stream data consumers. The term "talker" is used interchangeably with "stream data producer" to describe this role. The producer's function is central to enabling real-time data transmission in Ethernet-based systems. The definition emphasizes its role as a source of continuous, stream-oriented data output.

nodes.title: Stream data consumer
nodes.summary: A "stream data consumer" is an end node in an Ethernet network that continuously consumes data. It receives data through a stream, indicating ongoing, real-time data transmission. The term emphasizes the node's role as a recipient of continuous data flow rather than discrete packets. This definition aligns with its function in maintaining persistent data consumption in networked systems.

nodes.title: AVTP presentation time
nodes.summary: The AVTP presentation time, as defined in [1, IEEE1722], is the gPTP time when designated data in an AVTPDU payload is delivered to a time-sensitive application. It is encoded in the "avtp_timestamp" field of the AVTPDU-common-stream-header. The calculation is TavtpPresentationTime = TcurrentGlobalTime + TmaxTransitTime. Notably, this time does not account for format conversion or processing delays at the receiving application, as specified in [1, IEEE1722] figure 6.

nodes.title: Max transit time
nodes.summary: The "Max transit time" is defined by [1, IEEE1722] and is calculated as the worst-case transit time from a stream data producer (talker) to a stream data consumer (listener). The method requires selecting a max transit time that is greater than or equal to the largest worst-case transit time observed in the system. This ensures the chosen value accommodates all potential delays in data transmission between producers and consumers. The definition emphasizes the necessity of exceeding or matching the maximum possible delay to maintain reliable streaming. No additional parameters or exceptions are specified in the text.

nodes.title: Media clock
nodes.summary: The "Media clock" is defined by [1, IEEE1722] as an entity that generates a rate, such as a precise hardware clock operating at a constant rate (e.g., 48kHz). It serves as a timing reference for media synchronization. The media clock is hosted by the media clock provider, which manages its operation and ensures accurate timing. The definition emphasizes its role in maintaining consistent temporal alignment for media processing. The example of 48kHz illustrates a typical application of a constant-rate clock in audio or multimedia systems.

nodes.title: Media clock provider
nodes.summary: A media clock provider is an end node hosting a media clock that transmits an IEEE1722 stream of subtype CRF to one or more media clock consumers. This stream includes presentation timestamps aligned with the media clock rate, enabling synchronization. The IEEE1722 CRF stream serves as a clock reference format for timing coordination. The term "listener" is equivalent to "stream data consumer" in this context. The provider's role is critical for maintaining temporal alignment in distributed media systems.

nodes.title: Media clock consumer
nodes.summary: A "media clock consumer" is an end node receiving IEEE1722 streams of subtype CRF (clock reference format) from a media clock provider, using the encapsulated data to recover its media clock (e.g., PLL). It relies on the provider's CRF stream for synchronization. The "ACF-stream" refers to IEEE1722 streams of subtype TSCF (time-synchronous control format) or NTSCF (non-time-synchronous control format), which transport ACF-messages (e.g., ACF_CAN) within AVTPDU-payload (ACF-payload). These ACF-streams encapsulate bus frames for time-synchronized or non-time-synchronized control. The distinction between TSCF and NT

nodes.title: ACF-message
nodes.summary: The ACF-message, defined by IEEE 1722, encapsulates a bus frame (e.g., CAN) using a specific ACF-message type (e.g., ACF_CAN). It comprises an ACF-message-header and an ACF-message-payload. Multiple ACF-messages of varying types can aggregate into an ACF-payload, which is transmitted within a single ACF-stream. The encapsulation ensures compatibility with different bus types while maintaining structured data transport. The ACF-message type determines the format and handling of the encapsulated bus frame.

nodes.title: ACF-message-header
nodes.summary: The "ACF-message-header" is the initial segment of an ACF-message, comprising the first 7 bits that define the ACF-message type. Specific types, such as ACF_CAN and ACF_LIN, are defined in [1, IEEE1722]. The subsequent 9 bits in the header specify the length of the following ACF-message-payload. This structure ensures the header encodes both message type and payload size for protocol compliance. No additional fields or details are included in the header beyond these 16 bits.

nodes.title: ACF-message-payload
nodes.summary: The "ACF-message-payload" is defined by [1, IEEE1722] as the second component of an ACF-message. It carries data specific to the ACF-message type, which is encoded in the accompanying ACF-message-header. For instance, an ACF_CAN-payload encapsulates a CAN2.0 frame. The payload's structure and content are determined by the message type specified in the header. This division ensures separation of control information (header) from the actual data payload.

nodes.title: Input documents & related standards and norms
Further applicable specification
nodes.summary: The functional cluster adheres to AUTOSAR's core specification [3], with chapter 7.1 ("General requirements for all Functional Clusters") serving as an additional required specification. The **Raw Data Stream** functional cluster is governed by the general specification [4, RS General], which incorporates the SWS General as an additional required specification. Specific requirements for the **Raw Data Stream** functional cluster are detailed in [5, RS Communication Management]. All referenced specifications are part of the AUTOSAR framework.

nodes.title: Known limitations
nodes.summary: The current solution lacks runtime adaptability to network topology changes, such as service discovery, requiring RawDataStreams to be statically configured on the same ECU

structure.title: A **Raw Data Streams** using IEEE1722 protocol support the following IEEE1722 subtypes (specified by [1, IEEE1722]) as transport protocol: **AAF**, **61883_IIDC**, **RVF**, **CRF**, **TSCF** and NTSCF**. Support of other subtypes specified by [1, IEEE1722] may be added in future.
Dependencies to other Functional Clusters
structure.summary: This chapter outlines the dependencies of the functional cluster on other functional clusters, emphasizing that AUTOSAR does not standardize interfaces exclusive to inter-cluster communication to enable efficient, OS-dependent implementations. The primary objective is to provide an informative guideline for interactions between clusters without specifying syntactical details, ensuring compatibility across documents and supporting parallel development of distinct clusters. Internal interfaces, parameters, and return values are left to platform providers, with the possibility of additional internal interfaces being defined. A detailed technical architecture of the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform is referenced in [6].

nodes.title: Provided Interfaces
nodes.summary: The "Provided Interfaces" section outlines the public interfaces available from this functional cluster to facilitate interaction with other functional clusters. It serves as an overview of the APIs or communication endpoints exposed by the cluster for integration purposes. These interfaces define the methods and protocols through which external clusters can interact with this cluster's functionalities. The documentation emphasizes their role in enabling interoperability and system coordination. No additional technical details beyond the interface scope are

nodes.title: Required Interfaces
nodes.summary: The "Required Interfaces" section outlines the public interfaces needed by the Raw Data Stream functional cluster from other clusters in the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform. It specifies three required interfaces: **CryptoStack** (from Cryptography) for encrypted connections, **Logger** (from Log and Trace) for standardized message logging, and **SynchronizedTimeBaseConsumer** (from Time Synchronization) for IEEE1722-based time synchronization. These interfaces enable critical functionalities such as secure communication, logging, and time coordination. The section references Figure 5.1 and Table 5.2 for detailed interface requirements, though the initial table is empty.

structure.title: Requirements Tracing
structure.summary: The "Requirements Tracing" section

nodes.title: Raw Data Streaming using IP based protocols (network layer)
Raw Data Streaming Interface
nodes.summary: The Raw Data Streaming Interface operates synchronously with blocking behavior by default, but timeout handling is required to return errors if operations exceed specified thresholds, with timeout parameters defined per operation. Configuration involves specifying credentials and socket parameters via RawDataStreamMapping and EthernetRawDataStreamMapping, as detailed in TPS_ManifestSpecification [8]. Security is configurable through TLS, IPSec, and IAM access control, all managed within the deployment and mapping model of the interface, per TPS_ManifestSpecification [8]. The API supports both client and server modes, with Figure 7.1 illustrating the logical usage of RawDataStream instances.

nodes.title: Use cases
nodes.summary: The RawDataStream interface supports three use cases: (1) client connecting to a non-AUTOSAR sensor via socket, (2) server waiting for a connection from a non-AUTOSAR sensor, and (3) client/server communication with another AUTOSAR RawDataStream instance. Socket connections can use UDP or TCP protocols with Unicast or Multicast configurations. Figure 7.2 illustrates the currently supported use cases and the corresponding Deployment model artifacts required for configuration. Ethernet socket parameters

nodes.title: Access Control via IAM
nodes.summary: The section outlines access control restrictions for RawDataStream operations, emphasizing that the **InstanceSpecifier** provided during **RawDataStreamClient::Create()** or **RawDataStreamServer::Create()** must reference a **RPortPrototype** mapped via **RawDataStreamMapping** to the requesting process. If invalid, it constitutes a violation. Operations like **Connect**, **WaitForConnection**, **Read**, and **Write** allow a timeout parameter; absence of a timeout causes blocking. If a

nodes.title: Secure Communication
nodes.summary: Raw data streams can be transported via TCP and UDP, enabling the use of various security mechanisms to secure communication. The supported security protocols include TLS 1.2 (RFC5246), DTLS 1.2 (RFC6347), and IPSec. These protocols provide encryption and integrity protection for data transmitted over the network. Each protocol is referenced by its respective RFC standard, ensuring compatibility and adherence to defined

nodes.title: Creation and use of secure channels for Raw Data Streaming
nodes.summary: The Raw Data Stream software must create secure UDP and TCP channels as specified by TLSSecureComProps within

structure.title: A (D)TLS secure channel may provide authenticity, integrity and confidentiality which may be used with raw data streaming.
structure.summary: The TLS and DTLS implementations must support specific cipher suites: TLS_PSK_WITH_NULL_SHA256 for authentication (RFC5487) and TLS_PSK_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 for confidentiality (RFC5487). A TLS secure channel is required when a `tlsSecureComProps` instance in an `EthernetRawDataStreamMapping` is configured for TCP transmission via `localTcpPort`. Similarly, a DTLS secure channel is mandated for UDP transmission using `localUdpPort` in the same

nodes.title: Raw Data Stream Interfaces
nodes.summary: The "Raw Data Stream Interfaces" section outlines the technical specifications and protocols for handling raw data streams, detailing their structure, transmission methods, and integration requirements. It references chapter 7.1.1.1 for comprehensive information on supported use cases, which include scenarios such as real-time data processing, storage optimization, and system interoperability. The interfaces are designed to ensure compatibility across different data sources and processing pipelines. Specific implementation details, such as data formatting and error-handling mechanisms, are covered in the referenced chapter. This section serves as a foundational reference for developers and system architects working with raw data stream architectures.

structure.title: A socket shall be created and bound to the address and port specified in the local credentials. In case of TCP it shall also mark the socket as passive and listen for connections (for use case 1:1 TCP unicast).
structure.summary: The section describes socket creation and configuration based on server credentials. For TCP, a socket is created, bound to the specified address and port, marked as passive, and set to listen for connections (1:1 TCP unicast). For UDP, if Remote Unicast Credentials are defined, an endpoint is created and stored in the **Raw-DataStreamServer** object for 1:1 un

nodes.title: Set up a RawDataStream connection
nodes.summary: The **RawDataStreamClient::Connect()** function establishes a unicast socket connection for the RawDataStream instance, connecting to a TCP server, while UDP does not require a connection and restricts packets to the specified address. Socket endpoints and attributes are defined in the manifest via the InstanceSpecifier constructor, with TLS/DTLS initialized if configured. For TCP, the constructor prepares the socket to accept connections, and **WaitForConnection()** waits for an incoming request. In UDP, no connection is established, and **WaitForConnection()** returns immediately without action. Upstream requirements RS_CM_00410, RS_CM_00411, RS_CM_004

nodes.title: Read data from a RawDataStream connection
nodes.summary: The `RawDataStreamClient::ReadData()` function reads data from either a multicast socket (if Multicast Credentials are defined) or a unicast TCP/UDP socket, depending on the connection type. It leverages zero-copy semantics via `std::unique_ptr` to transfer ownership of the read data to the application through `ReadDataResult.data`. Similarly, `RawDataStreamServer::ReadData()` reads data from the unicast socket associated with the `RawDataStream` instance, using the same zero-copy mechanism for efficiency. Both functions handle data retrieval based on the connection type (multicast vs. unicast TCP/UDP) as specified in the upstream requirements RS_CM_00410, RS_CM_00411, RS_CM_00412.

nodes.title: Write data to a RawDataStream connection
nodes.summary: The `RawDataStreamClient::WriteData()` function writes bytes to a socket connection for a defined RawDataStream instance. If multicast credentials are defined for the client, a single socket handles both multicast reading and unicast writing (for 1:N UDP + 1:1 UDP use cases). For 1:N scenarios with server-side multicast credentials, data is sent via the multicast socket created in the constructor; otherwise, TCP data uses the unicast socket from `Wait

structure.title: Raw Data Streaming using IEEE1722 protocol (data link layer)

nodes.title: Raw Data Streaming Interface
nodes.summary: The Raw Data Streaming Interface operates via IEEE1722 protocol with synchronous, blocking operation calls. Configuration involves specifying data link layer socket parameters for IEEE1722 streams using **RawDataStreamMapping** and **EthernetMacRawDataStreamMapping**, as detailed in the _TPS_ManifestSpecification_ [8]. Security is achieved through MACSec in middleware and IAM access control, both configurable in the deployment and mapping model. Applications can act as either **IEEE1722 stream consumers** or **producers** via the Raw Data Streaming API. Figure 7

nodes.title: Use cases
nodes.summary: The Raw data stream interface for IEEE1722 supports four communication mappings via data link layer sockets: 1:1 (one producer, one consumer, e.g., video stream), 1:n (one producer, multiple consumers, e.g., audio stream), n:1 (multiple producers, one consumer, e.g., sensor fusion), and n:m (multiple producers, multiple consumers, e.g., CAN frames across buses). These mappings enable IEEE1722 streams to connect producers and consumers through socket-based data link layer communication, with examples spanning video, audio, sensor data, and networked CAN frames. The interface facilitates both unicast and multicast scenarios, ensuring data transmission aligns with IEEE1722 protocol requirements. Each use case specifies distinct producer-consumer relationships and application contexts.

nodes.summary: IEEE1722RawDataStream communication is statically configured via the Deployment model and operates statelessly in a unid

nodes.summary: The section outlines an access control rule for IAM, specifying that if a `Process` invokes the `IEEE1722

nodes.title: Secure Communication
nodes.summary: The IEEE1722RawDataStream communication can be secured using the MACSec protocol at the data link layer. This security mechanism is outside the scope of the Raw Data Stream functional cluster. Implementation of MACSec is required to be established by the system itself. The text specifies that the functional cluster does not handle or manage this security mechanism. No additional protocols or details are mentioned regarding the secure communication setup.

nodes.title: Consume IEEE1722 stream data
nodes.summary: The ReadData operation of an IEEE1722RawDataStreamConsumer instance must request to read at most `maxNumberOfDatagrams` from the connected IEEE1722RawDataStream, with the result being a buffer containing only datagrams where AVTPDU-header inspection succeeded (as described in 7.2

nodes.title: AVTPDU-header inspection
nodes.summary: The AVTPDU-header inspection ensures consistency between received AVTPDU-header fields and the configuration of the IEEE1722RawDataStreamConsumer instance during the ReadData operation. It evaluates headers specified in [1, IEEE1722], including AVTPDU-common-header, AVTPDU-common-stream-header, and AVTPDU-alternative-header, while excluding AVTPDU-common-control-header due to unsupported usage in AUTOSAR. Consistency checks include verifying subtype and version field matches with configured values, ensuring timestamps are not ahead of synchronized global time, and validating sequence

nodes.title: Produce IEEE1722 stream data
nodes.summary: To produce IEEE1722 stream data, an application must create an `IEEE1722RawDataStreamProducer` instance via the template class `ara::rds::IEEE1722RawDataStreamProducer`, specifying the IEEE1722 subtype. This instance represents the configuration for a specific stream, derived into its member variables during creation. Data is provided as an `ara::core::Vector` of datagrams, where each element contains stream data (e.g., length, payload) and must match the producer's type; mismatches result in datagrams being skipped. Applications must also instantiate a local class corresponding to the IEEE1722 subtype, setting all non-optional member variables for each datagram.

structure.title: of 185 Document ID 1098: AUTOSAR_AP_SWS_RawDataStream
structure.summary: The IEEE1722RawDataStreamProducer's WriteData operation processes ara::core::Vector datagrams, each containing mandatory and optional IEEE1722 header fields.

nodes.title: AVTPDU-header creation
nodes.summary: The AVTPDU-header creation ensures consistency and completeness of field values provided by the application against the **IEEE1722RawDataStreamProducer** configuration during the **WriteData** operation. It adheres to the AVTPDU-header formats specified in [1, IEEE1722], which include AVTPDU-common-header, DVTPDU-common-stream-header, DVTPDU-common-control-header, and AVTPDU-alternative-header. Shared fields across formats, such as stream ID, are detailed in Chapter 7.2.4.2.1.1, while subsequent sub-chapters outline specific field settings for each header type. The **AVTPDU-common-control-header** is excluded, as supported **IEEE1722 streams** in AUT

nodes.title: AVTPDU-common-header fields
nodes.summary: The AVTPDU

nodes.title: AVTPDU-common-stream-header fields
nodes.summary: The AVTPDU-common-stream-header fields are defined by specific rules for IEEE1722 streams. The **mr (media clock restart)** field is set to the application-provided value if valid, otherwise zero. The **sv (stream_id valid)** field is always set to 1. The **sequence_num** is initialized to the current value, and the **tu (timestamp uncertain

nodes.title: AVTPDU-alternative-header fields
nodes.summary: The AVTPDU-alternative-header fields are specific to AVTP stream data subtypes and are detailed in the corresponding subchapters for IEEE1722 streams. These subchapters apply to the AVTP stream data subtypes **CRF** and **NTSCF**. The fields are defined within the context of these subtypes, ensuring subtype-specific configurations. The text references subchapters but does not specify their exact content beyond their association with CRF and NTSCF.

nodes.title: 61883_IHDC-header fields
nodes.summary: The section outlines the configuration of AVTPDU-header fields for IEEE1722 streams with AVTP stream data subtype "61883_IIDC," based on the tag field value. When the tag is 0, specific fields (gv, gateway_info, tag, channel, tc code, sy) are set to configured values from the IEEE1722RawDataStreamProducer instance, while common headers are retained

nodes.title: AAF-header fields
nodes.summary: The section outlines the requirements for setting specific header fields in an AVTPDU-header when an IEEE1722 stream uses the AAF (AVTP Audio Format) subtype. For general AAF streams, the **format**, **sp**, and **evt** fields must be set to values from the **IEEE1722RawDataStreamProducer** instance (referenced via SWS_RDS_90262, 90263, 90264). If the format indicates AAF PCM, additional fields like **nsr**, **channels_per_frame**, **bit_depth**, and **pcm_data_payload** are configured from the same instance (SWS_RDS_90265–90267). For AAF AES3 format, fields such as **nfr**, **streams_per_frame**, and AES3-specific parameters (**aes3_data_type_h**, **aes3_dt_ref**, **aes3_data_type

nodes.title: ACF-header fields
nodes.summary: The "ACF-header fields" section outlines the creation of AVTP headers for ACF (AVTP Control Format) data subtypes NTSCF and TSCF, per IEEE1722 standards. AUTOSAR does not support SRP, so

nodes.title: CRF-header fields
nodes.summary: The "CRF-header fields" section outlines the requirements for setting specific AVTPDU-header fields when creating an IEEE1722 stream with AVTP data subtype "CRF" (Clock Reference Format), as defined in IEEE1722 chapter 10.4. Key fields include `sv`, `mr`, `fs`, `tu`, `sequence_num`, `type`, `stream

structure.title: of 185 Document ID 1098: AUTOSAR\_AP\_SWS\_RawDataStream
structure.summary: The section clarifies that fields from IEEE1722's "10.4.13 crf_data field" reside in the `crf_data` field of the CRF-payload, managed by an application. It specifies that the Raw DataStream functional cluster excludes specific types: User-specified type, Audio sample type, Video frame sync type, Video line sync type, and Machine cycle type. Additionally, it directs reference to Chapter 7.2.4.2.1.2 for details on AVTPDU-common-header fields. The note emphasizes that these excluded fields are outside the scope of the Raw DataStream functional cluster. All technical details from the original text are preserved without extrapolation.

nodes.title: RVF-header fields
nodes.summary: The "RVF-header fields" section outlines the requirements for setting specific header fields in AVTPDU headers for IEEE1722 streams with RVF subtype, as defined in IEEE1722 chapter 12.2. Fields like active_pixels, total_lines, ap, f, ef, evt, pd, i, pixel_depth, pixel_format, frame_rate, colorspace, num_lines, i_seq_num, and line_number must be populated either by application-provided values (defaulting to 0 if unavailable) or from the IEEE1722RawDataStreamProducer instance's configured values. Each field's configuration is tied to specific S

nodes.title: Error handling for consuming or producing IEEE1722 stream data
IEEE1722RawDataStream
nodes.summary: The IEEE1722RawDataStream section outlines two key behaviors for handling operations under specific conditions. If a ReadData or WriteData operation is invoked when the data link layer socket is disconnected, the operation must do nothing and return `kStreamAlreadyDisconnected`. Additionally,

nodes.title: Functional cluster lifecycle
nodes.summary: The "Functional cluster lifecycle" section outlines the behavior of a functional cluster throughout its operational lifecycle. It references general behavior definitions for `ara::core::Initialize` and `ara::core::Deinitiize` specified in [SWS_CORE_15005] and [SWS_CORE_90022] from [3]. These standards define the expected execution and termination processes for the cluster's lifecycle management. The section emphasizes adherence to these standardized behaviors for consistent system operation. No additional implementation-specific details are provided beyond the referenced specifications.

nodes.title: Startup
nodes.summary: The Raw Data Stream functional cluster requires no special startup handling. No state is maintained across power cycles, meaning the system does not retain prior operational data or configurations after shutdown. This design ensures minimal initialization overhead and independence between sessions.

nodes.title: Security Events
nodes.summary: The "Security Events" section explicitly states that the functional cluster does not define any security events. No event types, triggers, or associated actions are specified for this cluster. The absence of security event definitions implies no monitoring, alerting, or response mechanisms are configured within this context. This documentation confirms the lack of security-related event handling in the described functional cluster. The text provides no further details or exceptions regarding security event definitions.

nodes.title: Violation Messages
nodes.summary:
The "Violation Messages" section outlines four specific error types with unique identifiers and descriptions:  
1. **InstanceSpecifierMappingIntegrityViolation (0x8

nodes.title: Production Errors
nodes.summary: The "Production Errors" section states that the functional cluster does not define any production errors (Diagnostic Events). It specifies a message ID of 0x80001ff9 with a message type of DLT_LOG_FATAL. The associated arguments include: **modeledProcess ld** (UTF-8 uint8, meta-model identifier of the violating process), **location** (UTF-8 uint8, implementation-defined identifier of the violation source), **instanceSpecifier** (UTF-8 uint8, used to create the object), and **className** (UTF-8 uint8, name of the instantiated class). All arguments are encoded as UTF-8 uint8.

structure.title: API specification
structure.summary: This section serves as a reference for APIs defined by the functional cluster. The API details are presented in tables within subsequent chapters. Section 8.1 provides an explanation of the structure and content of these API tables. The documentation emphasizes that the tables describe the APIs' specifications. No additional technical details beyond the referenced chapters are included.

nodes.title: Class: RawDataStreamClient
nodes.summary: The **RawDataStreamClient** class is defined in the `ara::rds` namespace, implementing the `RawDataStreamClientInterface` for managing binary data streams over network connections. It is declared in the header file `#include "ara/rds/raw_data_stream.h"` and relies on the forwarding header `#include "ara/rds

nodes.title: sequenceNum
nodes.summary: The `sequenceNum` variable, defined in the `ara::rds::IEEE1722DetagramAVTP-DUCCommonStreamHeaderFields` namespace, is a 32-bit unsigned integer used to track the sequence of data packets in a stream. It ensures ordered delivery and detects duplicate or missing packets by assigning a unique incrementing value to each transmission. The field is part of the common header for IEEE 1722 Detagram AVTP streams, facilitating reliable data flow management. Its value is incremented sequentially for each packet, enabling synchronization and error detection mechanisms. The variable is critical for maintaining stream integrity in audio-video transport protocols.

nodes.title: Class: RdsException
nodes.summary: The `RdsException` class, defined in the `ara::rds` namespace, inherits from `ara::core::Exception` and is used to represent exceptions thrown by the Raw Data Streams (RDS) module. It is declared in the header file `ara/rds/rds_error_domain.h` and requires inclusion of the forwarding header `ara/rds/rds_fwd.h`. The `ThrowAsException` method, part of the `RdsErrorDomain` class, converts an `ara::core::Error` object into a C++ exception, which is thread-safe but not exception-safe. This method does not participate in overload resolution when C++ exceptions are disabled, as specified by [SWS_CORE_10304].
